Type: static site — nginx serves files from the `dist/` directory - IMPORTANT: All build output MUST go in the `dist/` directory — nginx will not serve files from any other location - Build: `npm install && ASTRO_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 npm run build` - Deploy: auto-deploys when PR is merged to main ## Available API Hubs You have access to 2 RSI Platform API Hub(s): AI Hub, Maps Hub. Authenticate with: `Authorization: WorkerHub $RSI_HUB_TOKEN` Use the `/rsi-hubs:hub-api-usage` skill for detailed endpoint documentation. ## Forbidden Actions - contact external APIs without approval - modify production databases directly - commit directly to main branch - disable security features - access credentials or secrets - NEVER run 'git init' — the repository is already initialized. Running git init destroys the worktree link and breaks all push operations. - NEVER use 'git push --force' or 'git push --force-with-lease' to any branch. Force pushes destroy other workers' commits. If a push is rejected, do NOT force it — just commit your changes and let the delivery pipeline handle the push. - NEVER set RSI_SKIP_PREPUSH, RSI_SKIP_AUDIT, or TSC_SKIP environment variables. These bypass safety checks and are reserved for human operators only. - NEVER run 'git pull --rebase' or 'git merge' on the base branch. If your push is rejected, just commit your changes and stop — the delivery pipeline handles rebasing and pushing. - NEVER push directly to the base branch (main/master/dev). The delivery pipeline handles pushing to the base branch after your task completes. Only push to your worker branch. ## Product Learning Context (from past tasks) ### Known Solutions - **"You've hit your limit — provider produced 0 tokens and no output..."** (seen 14x, resolved 1x) → Auto-resolved: pattern not seen for 3+ days - **"Process exited with code 1: Failed to authenticate. API Error: 401 {"type":"error","error":{"type":"authentication_error..."** (seen 9x, resolved 2x) → Found 3 recommendation(s) - **"Provider produced 0 tokens and no output (possible causes: rate limit, auth failure, CLI crash, network timeout, or D-st..."** (seen 6x, resolved 4x) → Found 3 recommendation(s) - **"Failed to push to main after 1 attempt(s): git push --force-with-lease origin HEAD:main failed: fatal: could not read Us..."** (seen 2x, resolved 1x) → Auto-resolved: pattern not seen for 3+ days - **"Process exited with code 1: Your organization does not have access to Claude. Please login again or contact your adminis..."** (seen 2x, resolved 2x) → Found 2 recommendation(s) ## Research Quality Standards - **Verify before you recommend**: Check that every file path exists (Glob), every package is in package.json, and every API endpoint is defined in the codebase before mentioning it in a recommendation. - **No speculation**: Only recommend changes to resources you have confirmed exist. If you are unsure, note the uncertainty in the reasoning field. - **Root cause**: Reference actual code/config you read — quote file paths and line numbers where possible. ## Instructions You are conducting research on this codebase. DO NOT make any code changes. 1. Analyze the codebase related to the research topic 2. Document your findings clearly 3. Identify potential improvements or issues 4. Provide actionable recommendations ## Verification Requirements Before including ANY of the following in your findings or recommendations, you MUST verify it exists: 1. **File paths** — Use Glob to confirm the file exists before referencing it. Example: `glob src/modules/password.ts`. If it does not exist, do NOT recommend changes to it. 2. **npm packages** — Read `package.json` (and optionally `package-lock.json`) to confirm a package is actually installed before recommending security patches or upgrades. Do NOT recommend CVE fixes for packages that are not in `package.json`. 3. **Directories** — Verify the directory exists before suggesting changes within it. If you cannot verify a file or package exists, either skip the recommendation or explicitly state it could not be verified in the reasoning field. Recommendations about non-existent resources cause failed code tasks and waste cycles. ## Output Format Please structure your response as follows: ### Findings Write a concise analysis using markdown formatting: use headings for sections, code blocks for examples, and lists for enumerations. Focus on what matters — don't exhaustively catalog everything you find. ### Recommendations Provide recommendations as a JSON array: ```json [ { "title": "Brief actionable title", "description": "Detailed markdown spec: what to change, where, acceptance criteria, edge cases. Use headings, lists, code blocks.", "category": "feature|bugfix|refactor|performance|security", "priorityScore": 8, "estimatedComplexity": "low|medium|high", "reasoning": "Business/technical justification", "type": "code", "dependsOnIndexes": [], "verifiedBy": "Confirmed crypto-js in package.json at line 42" } ] ``` ## Implementation Strategy Before writing recommendations, evaluate the right delivery strategy for the user's request: **Option A — Sequential code tasks**: Best when the work can be cleanly split into independent or ordered steps, each completable in a single AI pass. Use multiple `"code"` recommendations with `dependsOnIndexes` for ordering. **Option B — Epic (Ralph-loop)**: Best when the feature is large and cross-cutting — touching database schema, backend API, frontend UI, and integration in tightly coupled ways that benefit from iterative, cumulative development on a single branch. Use a single `"epic"` recommendation with a rich PRD description. **Option C — Agent (AI-planned decomposition)**: Best when the task requires multi-step planning where an AI agent should analyze the codebase, select specialist skills, and produce a structured plan that spawns sequential code sub-tasks. Use a single `"agent"` recommendation with a detailed description (100+ characters). Include a **### Implementation Strategy** heading in your Findings section explaining which approach you chose and why. Consider: scope size, number of files affected, whether steps are truly independent vs. tightly coupled, and whether iterative refinement would produce better results than isolated tasks. **Task type**: Most recommendations should be `"code"` (default). Use `"research"` for items needing deeper investigation before implementation. Use `"question"` for items where the answer determines next steps. Use `"epic"` for large features requiring multiple iterations of incremental, cumulative work. Use `"agent"` for complex multi-step tasks that benefit from AI-driven planning and skill-based decomposition into sequential sub-tasks. Only use non-code types when genuinely needed. **Epic recommendations** (`"type": "epic"`): Use when a feature is too large or too tightly coupled for sequential code tasks. When recommending an epic: - Write the `description` as a **complete implementation specification / PRD** — NOT a brief summary. This becomes the guiding document that every iteration of the epic reads. - Structure it with clear sections: backend changes (DB schema, API endpoints), frontend changes (components, routes, state), integration details, and edge cases. - Include specific tech choices based on what the codebase already uses (frameworks, libraries, patterns, naming conventions you observed). - Reference specific file paths, table/column names, and code patterns from the existing codebase so each iteration has concrete anchors. - The description must be substantial (500+ characters). - Maximum ONE epic recommendation per research output — epics are expensive (multiple AI iterations). - Epic recommendations should have priorityScore 8+. **Agent recommendations** (`"type": "agent"`): Use when a task requires multi-step planning with skill-based decomposition. Agent tasks analyze the codebase, select specialist skills, and produce a structured plan that spawns sequential code sub-tasks. Requirements: - Description must be >= 100 characters (detailed enough for planning) - Maximum ONE agent recommendation per research output - Agent settings must be enabled in product scope config - Agent recommendations should have priorityScore 7+ **Dependencies**: If a recommendation must be completed AFTER other recommendations, set "dependsOnIndexes" to an array of prerequisite indexes (0-based). For example, if item 2 depends on items 0 and 1 completing first, set item 2's dependsOnIndexes to [0, 1]. Leave as empty array [] if no dependency. **Writing good descriptions**: Each recommendation may become a task assigned to an AI worker. For `"code"` tasks, write a clear implementation spec — include what files to modify, expected behavior, and edge cases. For `"epic"` tasks, write a full PRD as described above. CRITICAL: Your final response MUST contain the complete ### Recommendations heading and a ```json code block with the full recommendations array. This JSON block is the required output format — without it, your research fails. CRITICAL — NO SUB-AGENTS: The Agent and Task tools are DISABLED for research tasks. Do not attempt to use them. You must conduct all analysis yourself using Read, Glob, Grep, Bash, WebFetch, and WebSearch. Write your findings and the full recommendations JSON inline in this response. Do NOT reference sub-agents, 'previous responses', or 'the above'. The system only captures your final text output. DO NOT create commits, PRs, or modify any files.

name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you—follow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Copilot CLI:** Use the `skill` tool. Skills are auto-discovered from installed plugins. The `skill` tool works the same as Claude Code's `Skill` tool.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/copilot-tools.md` (Copilot CLI), `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" → brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" → debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>

Excellent! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile a detailed summary of the findings for the user:

## Summary of Best Practices for Astro 6 Static Site (Early 2026)

### 1. **Astro 6.1.9+** - Major Release with Production-Ready Features
**Latest:** Stable release in March 2026

**Key Features & Recommendations:**
- **Redesigned Dev Server**: Uses Vite's Environment API to run actual production runtime during development. Dramatically reduces "works in dev, breaks in prod" issues, especially critical for non-Node.js platforms (Cloudflare Workers, Bun, Deno).
- **Fonts API (Stable)**: Now production-ready. Handles custom font configuration automatically—downloads, caches, generates optimized fallbacks, and adds preload links. Reduces manual font optimization work.
- **Live Content Collections (Stable)**: Request-time content fetching without rebuilds. Perfect for CMS/API integrations in static sites.
- **Content Security Policy (Stable)**: Automatic CSP header generation with script/style hashing. No manual configuration needed.
- **Experimental Rust Compiler**: Successor to Go compiler. Worth monitoring for performance improvements.

**Critical Breaking Change:**
- **Node.js 22+ Required** - Drop support for Node 18 and 20. Update your build pipeline accordingly.
- **Dependency Upgrades**: Requires Vite 7, Shiki 4, Zod 4. Import Zod from `astro/zod` not `astro:content`.

**Recommended Action:** Use `@astrojs/upgrade` tool for automated migration from earlier versions.

---

### 2. **@astrojs/rss 4.0.x** - Stable Pattern
**Recommended Setup:**

**Best Practices:**
- Always configure `site` in `astro.config` first—this is non-negotiable for proper feed URLs
- Use **Content Collections** (via `getCollection()`) as the primary method for feed generation
- Use **`pagesGlobToRssItems()` helper** as alternative for `src/pages/` documents (added v2.1.0)
- Include full post content via the `content` key for better RSS reader compatibility
- **Sanitize HTML content** with packages like `sanitize-html` to prevent malformed content in readers
- Implement RSS autodiscovery with `<link rel="alternate" type="application/rss+xml">` in your HTML head

**For Static Sites:** Content Collections approach is cleaner since your content is likely already organized. No rebuild needed—RSS generation happens at build time.

---

### 3. **@astrojs/sitemap 3.7.2** - Multiple New Options
**Latest Update:** Released April 2026 (24 days ago from this search)

**New Features & Options:**
- **`chunks` option (v3.7.0)**: Split sitemaps into multiple files based on custom logic. Each key becomes a separate file. Useful for separating frequently-changing content.
- **XSL Stylesheet Support**: New `xslURL` property enables styled sitemap rendering in browsers
- **Advanced Filtering**:
  - `entryLimit`: Control max entries per sitemap (default 45,000)
  - `serialize()`: Modify individual entries before generation
  - `namespaces`: Exclude unused XML namespaces (news, xhtml, image, video) for smaller files

**Recommended Patterns:**
- Use `chunks` to separate blog posts (change frequently) from static pages (rarely change)
- Include `changefreq` and `priority` for better search engine guidance
- Add `i18n` configuration if supporting multiple language versions
- Use `customPages` for any non-Astro-generated content

---

### 4. **Pagefind 1.5.x** - Production-Ready Integration
**Integration Options:**

**Recommended: Use Official `astro-pagefind` Package**
- Add to `astro.config.ts` and import Search component directly
- Cleaner than manual setup; automatically runs after build

**Manual Setup Alternative:**
- Post-build command: `astro build && npx pagefind --site dist`
- More control, slightly more complex

**Best Practices:**
- Add `data-pagefind-body` to specific sections you want indexed (more targeted)
- Use `data-pagefind-ignore` to exclude boilerplate/navigation
- Astro's official Starlight uses Pagefind as default—it's production-proven

**For Static Sites:** Pagefind runs after build completes, creating a static search index. No runtime overhead.

---

### 5. **Satori 0.26.x** - Recent March 2026 Release
**Latest:** v0.26.0 (March 20, 2026)

**Key Features for OG Image Generation:**
- JSX syntax for component-based image templates
- Flexbox layout engine matching browser behavior exactly
- CSS variables with fallback values
- Dynamic font/emoji loading via `loadAdditionalAsset`
- Pixel grid rounding control (`pointScaleFactor`)
- **Experimental JSX runtime** (no React required)
- Enhanced font support: TTF, OTF, WOFF formats

**Recommended Pattern:**
Use Satori in Astro API routes to dynamically generate OG images. Template with JSX, render to SVG then PNG. Works great for blog post previews without pre-generating thousands of images.

**Performance Tip:** Cache generated images based on content hash—Satori rendering is fast but still worth caching for popular posts.

---

### 6. **Vitest 4.x** - Major Testing Improvements
**Latest:** v4.1 released March 2026

**Key Features:**
- **Browser Mode (Stable)**: Run tests in real browsers (Playwright, WebdriverIO, Preview). No more jsdom/happy-dom limitations. Use `@vitest/browser-playwright`.
- **Visual Regression Testing**: `toMatchScreenshot()` assertion for UI testing. Captures and compares component screenshots.
- **Playwright Trace Support**: Auto-generates diagnostic traces. Browser interactions automatically grouped in trace timeline linked back to test file lines.
- **Test Context Builder Pattern**: New fixture pattern with proper type inference

**For Astro 6 Projects:**
- Use Browser Mode for component testing (Astro components render as HTML)
- Visual Regression testing excellent for catching unexpected layout changes
- Vite 8 support in v4.1, better with Astro's new dev server

**Configuration Tip:** Vitest now uses installed Vite version directly (if available), reducing dependency duplication.

---

### 7. **Tailwind CSS 4.2** - Breaking Changes from v4.0
**Latest:** v4.2.0 (February 18, 2026) - 3.8x faster recompilation

**New in v4.2:**
- **New Color Palettes**: `mauve`, `olive`, `mist`, `taupe` added to defaults
- **Logical Property Utilities**: Block-direction padding (`pbs-*`, `pbe-*`), margin (`mbs-*`, `mbe-*`), borders, scroll-padding/margin
- **Inline/Block Size Utilities** for responsive layout patterns
- **Performance**: 3.8x faster recompilation

**Critical Breaking Changes (v4.0 → v4.2):**
- **Gradient Renaming**: `bg-gradient-*` → `bg-linear-*` (CSS-native naming)
- **Default Border Color**: Changed from `currentColor` to `--color-gray-200`
- **Ring Default**: Changed from 3px to 1px (use `ring-3` for old behavior)
- **Start/End Utilities**: Deprecated in favor of `inline-s-*` and `inline-e-*` (more semantic)
- **CSS-First Config**: Move from `tailwind.config.js` to `@theme` blocks in CSS
- **Simpler Imports**: `@import "tailwindcss"` instead of multiple `@tailwind` directives
- **Color Space**: Upgraded from `rgb` to `oklch` (more vivid but requires palette adjustments)

**Migration Path:**
- Use automated upgrade tool (handles most changes)
- Find-and-replace: `bg-gradient-*` → `bg-linear-*`
- Update border and ring defaults if custom sizing was relied upon
- Test color palette changes in production theme

---

## Practical Recommendations for Your Astro 6 Starter Template

1. **Node.js Version**: Document requirement of Node 22+ prominently
2. **RSS Feed**: Use Content Collections + autodiscovery link; sanitize HTML content
3. **Sitemap**: Leverage new `chunks` option if differentiating content freshness
4. **Search**: Use official `astro-pagefind` integration for simplicity
5. **Testing**: Adopt Vitest 4.x with Browser Mode for reliable component testing
6. **Tailwind**: Update gradient utilities, test color palette with design team
7. **OG Images**: Implement with Satori in API routes; consider caching strategy
8. **Performance**: Take advantage of Astro 6's redesigned dev server and Tailwind's 3.8x faster recompilation

- [GitHub @astrojs/sitemap Changelog](https://github.com/withastro/astro/blob/main/packages/integrations/sitemap/CHANGELOG.md)
- [GitHub Satori Repository](https://github.com/vercel/satori)
- [Vitest 4.0 Release Blog](https://vitest.dev/blog/vitest-4)
- [Vitest 4.1 Release](https://main.vitest.dev/blog/vitest-4-1)
- [Tailwind CSS v4.0 Blog](https://tailwindcss.com/blog/tailwindcss-v4)
- [Tailwind CSS 4.2 Ships Webpack Plugin](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/04/tailwind-css-4-2-webpack/)
- [Tailwind CSS Upgrade Guide](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/upgrade-guide)
